one_line_takeaway: A first hands-on comparison of Garak, Giskard, PyRIT, and CyberSecEval showing that the real weak link in LLM vulnerability scanners is not attack coverage but the evaluator component, whose misclassification of attack success reaches a ~37% error rate for Garak while LLM-based evaluators (PyRIT, Giskard) are the most reliable.
key_finding: All four scanners' evaluators show notable, previously-unreported error rates in detecting successful attacks, and this is the paper's headline result. Garak (static string-matching evaluator) exhibits the worst reliability, with a maximum MOE of 26% corresponding to a 37% error rate in detecting successful attacks; PyRIT's LLM-based evaluators are the most reliable (lowest MOE), and Giskard's and PyRIT's LLM-based evaluators offer the highest reliability overall. On attack quality, static-focused Garak leads: it has the most attack types and highest instance counts, with ASR ranging from ~20% (context-and-continuation) to nearly 70%+ (insecure code). Qualitative analysis attributes evaluator errors to two root causes: static evaluators are over-simplistic and context-blind (e.g. flagging on the literal word 'unethical', or failing on a missing colon after 'ANTI-DAN'), while LLM-based evaluators are hard to control (e.g. a mis-generated 'requirement' causes a valid refusal to be scored invalid). The paper concludes there is a fundamental gap and recommends regulators standardize evaluation *of the evaluator component* and build a shared benchmarking platform for scanners.
core_contribution: The paper (from Fujitsu Research) presents what the authors claim is the first hands-on comparative study of four leading open-source LLM vulnerability scanners: Garak (Nvidia, v0.9.0.14.post1), Giskard (v2.14.4), PyRIT (Microsoft, v0.2.2.dev0), and CyberSecEval (Meta). Its own contributions are fourfold: (1) a 'unifying principles' abstraction that recasts all scanners as an automated red-teaming flow built from attacker-evaluator pairs, with attacks and evaluators each being either 'static' (predefined datasets / string-and-regex matching) or 'LLM-based'; (2) detailed per-scanner feature comparison (Table II) covering guardrails interface, multi-language support, customizable attacks, automated customization, evaluator explanations, and insecure-coding tests; (3) a quantitative evaluation of each scanner across four target LLMs; and (4) a foundational labeled dataset of manually annotated attack/response pairs to establish ground truth for measuring evaluator reliability. The paper's central new finding is a previously-unquantified reliability gap in the *evaluator* component of every scanner tested.
threat_model: The scanners under study red-team conversational LLMs by sending adversarial prompts through the natural-language prompt interface to elicit invalid responses. The attack surface spans five unified categories: jailbreak attacks (bypassing safety restrictions, e.g. DAN, AutoDAN, encoding tricks), gradient-based attacks (GCG greedy-coordinate-gradient suffixes), context-and-continuation attacks (eliciting bias/stereotypes, hallucinations, information/PII disclosure, harmful content), insecure-code-generation attacks (inducing malware/keyloggers or reproducing insecure coding practices/CWEs in generated code), and multi-turn attacks (sequential adversarial dialogue toward a goal). The paper itself does not propose a new attack; its 'threat' framing is the meta-problem that if a scanner's evaluator misjudges attack success, defenders get an unreliable picture of which vulnerabilities their model actually has.
limitations: The authors themselves note this is an H2-2024 'versions snapshot' of fast-moving tools, so specific numbers may age quickly (though they argue the identified gaps generalize). The labeled dataset is modest (1,045 pairs) and deliberately excludes ambiguous/subjective responses, which may understate real-world evaluator difficulty. Human-in-the-loop / semi-automatic scanner modes (e.g. PyRIT's converters) were declared out of scope. Attack coverage differs sharply across scanners and categories (e.g. Garak C&C had 2,852 prompts vs PyRIT multi-turn only 20; Giskard jailbreak only 90), so cross-scanner ASR/MOE comparisons rest on very uneven sample sizes. The forced grouping of ~35 heterogeneous attacks into a handful of unified categories reduces granularity, as the authors acknowledge. Definitions of 'invalid' response are partly subjective. The paper contributes no new defense or scanner, only an assessment and recommendations.

datasets_used: Authors' own foundational labeled dataset of 1,045 manually annotated adversarial attack/response pairs (referred to as a ~1,000-sample dataset), balanced across attack categories, ambiguous cases excluded
~5K adversarial prompts drawn from the four scanners' built-in test suites

Per-scanner labeled subsets: Garak (120 Dan-in-the-Wild-Mini jailbreak, 90 Goodside/Misleading C&C, 80 RealToxicityPrompts/Malwaregen code, 60 Knownbad-Signature gradient); PyRIT (120 single-step + 45 multi-step conversations); Giskard (all jailbreak pairs + 6 C&C tests, 35-prompt prompt-injection set); CyberSecEval (120 Prompt-Injection jailbreak, 140 Instruct/Autocomplete/Mitre code)
quantitative_results: Garak worst-case evaluator reliability: max MOE 26% = 37% error rate in detecting successful attacks
Garak Jailbreak (802 prompts) ASR/MOE: Command-R 73.00%/17.2%, LLaMA 3 8B 39.00%/10.7%, Mistral Small 59.00%/16.3%, GPT-4o 55.00%/16.8%
Garak Context-and-Continuation (2,852 prompts) ASR: Command-R 23%, LLaMA 21%, Mistral 25%, GPT-4o 11% (MOE up to 26.4%)
Garak Code-Gen (252 prompts) ASR: Command-R 74.30%, LLaMA 45.80%, Mistral 82.40%, GPT-4o 65.00%
Garak Gradient-based (41 prompts) ASR ~36-51% with very low MOE (0.05-0.2%)
PyRIT Jailbreak (310 prompts) ASR: Command-R 8%, LLaMA 7%, Mistral 30.04%, GPT-4o 9%; Multi-turn (20 prompts) ASR: Command-R 31%, LLaMA 9%, Mistral 19%, GPT-4o 45%
Giskard Jailbreak (90 prompts) ASR: Command-R 56.66%, LLaMA 10.0%, Mistral 17.78%, GPT-4o 7.78%; C&C (100 prompts) ASR: Command-R 40.0%, LLaMA 20.0%, Mistral 16.0%, GPT-4o 26.0%
CyberSecEval Code-Gen (757 prompts) ASR ~10-19.5%; CyberSecEval Jailbreak (251 prompts) ASR ~45-50.9%
Overall: 4 scanners x 4 LLMs evaluated with ~5K adversarial prompts
baselines_compared: The four scanners are compared against one another: Garak (Nvidia, static string-matching), Giskard (LLM-based + dual-context requirements), PyRIT (Microsoft, fully LLM-based, single- and multi-turn), CyberSecEval (Meta, pattern-matching Insecure Code Detector using Weggli/Semgrep/regex vs CWEs)
Manual human annotation of 1,045 attack/response pairs serves as the ground-truth baseline against which each scanner evaluator's MOE is measured
Four target LLMs used as the shared evaluation substrate: LLaMA 3 8B, Command-R, GPT-4o, Mistral Small

Supplementary appendix additionally surveys 12 other scanners (Prompt Fuzzer, HouYi, Dioptra, JailBreakingLLMs, LLMAttacks, PromptInject, Promptfoo, LLMCanary, Agentic Security, LLMFuzzer, PromptMap, Vigil-LLM) as context, not as quantitative baselines
